title: Polarization-Resolved Photon Statistics of Cavity Quantum Materials
authors: Benjamin Kass, Spenser Talkington, Martin Claassen
abstract: By forming hybrid light-matter states, optical cavities offer a route for engineering material properties, however, unambiguously probing the effects of light-matter coupling remains difficult. Here, we show that the polarization-resolved statistics of photons transmitted through a cavity, measurable via $g^{(2)}$, provide one such diagnostic. By relating $g^{(2)}$ to matter correlation functions such as the Raman structure factor, we link photon bunching and antibunching to material properties. By applying this method to the stripy-to-antiferromagnetic transition in the Kitaev-Heisenberg spin model, we find that polarization-dependent patterns of bunching and antibunching encode the magnetic point-group symmetries of each phase and characterize the behavior at the phase boundary. Finally, we predict measuring $g^{(2)}$ for output photon pairs polarized orthogonal to the input field will isolate higher-order light-matter scattering processes that probe higher-order material correlations.
summary: The paper demonstrates that measuring polarization-resolved photon statistics ($g^{(2)}$) transmitted through an optical cavity coupled to a quantum magnet can act as a sensitive probe of the underlying magnetic order. By analyzing how bunching and antibunching change with input/output polarizations, researchers can map out the magnetic point group symmetries across quantum phase transitions in models like the Kitaev-Heisenberg system.
main_problem: The study aims to develop a diagnostic method using polarization-resolved photon statistics ($g^{(2)}$) to unambiguously probe light-matter coupling effects and characterize magnetic properties in cavity quantum materials.
main_result: Polarization-dependent patterns of photon bunching/antibunching encode the magnetic point-group symmetries of different phases (e.g., $C_2$ vs $C_6$) and allow for probing higher-order material correlations via orthogonal polarization measurements.
method: The analysis uses Green's function formalism and perturbative expansions to calculate the two-photon correlation function, $g^{(2)}$, relating it directly to matter correlation functions like the Raman structure factor.
model_or_system: The system models an optical cavity coupled to quantum magnets described by extensions of the Kitaev-Heisenberg spin model (J-K-$\Gamma$ model) on lattices like honeycomb clusters. The Hamiltonian includes terms for Heisenberg, Kitaev, and general couplings.
key_observables: Polarization-resolved second-order photon correlation function ($g^{(2)}_{\mu\mu'}$), which exhibits bunching ($>1$) or antibunching ($<1$).
important_parameters: Coupling strength ($D$), input/output polarizations ($\mu, \mu'$), and magnetic parameters defining the phase (e.g., $	heta, \phi$ in J-K-$\Gamma$ model).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on perturbative expansions in the coupling strength $D$, often assuming a Markovian approximation for the cavity dynamics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phase diagrams, energy level shifts ($\delta E_n \propto n^2$), and polarization-dependent coincidence spectra ($g^{(2)}$) showing symmetry changes across Quantum Phase Transitions (QPTs).
paper_structure: The paper builds theoretically by establishing the link between $g^{(2)}$ and matter correlations, applying this to specific models like Kitaev/J-K-$\Gamma$, and detailing how polarization filtering isolates higher-order scattering processes sensitive to magnetic symmetries.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, non-invasive optical diagnostic tool for characterizing the complex ground state symmetries and correlation functions of strongly correlated quantum magnets, bridging cavity QED with condensed matter magnetism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Many-Body Photon Blockade. (a) The cavity-embedded Kitaev-Heisenberg spin model is a paradigmatic model hosting a rich set of competing magnetic phases that can hybridize with cavity photons. Classical ordering patterns of spins are shown for stripy and N´eel-antiferromagnet (AFM) phases, tuned via a mixing angle ϕ for Kitaev and Heisenberg interactions. (b) Energy levels of the n-photon sector ground states |gs, n⟩, with a single cavity mode. Without light-matter coupling, these states are equally separated by the cavity mode frequency ωc (green arrows). With light-matter coupling, the photons and material hybridize, causing energy shifts δEn ∝n2 that are non-linear in the photon...

FIG. 2. Polarization-Dependent Coincidence. Coinci- dence response in a cavity with two polarization modes as a function of the output photon linear polarization angle α in the plane of the material relative to the Kitaev z bonds. The top row shows the response to linearly polarized input pho- tons with the same angle α while the bottom row uses right circularly polarized input photons, as depicted in the overlaid diagrams. (a) and (c) Coincidence for the AFM phase at ϕ = 1.708π. (b) and (d) Coincidence for the stripy phase at ϕ = 1.7π, biased along Kitaev z bonds. These correspond to the gray dotted lines in Fig. 1(d). As in Fig. 1(d), γ = 0.1, g = 0.3, ω0 = 0.6, and B = 0.001. Green...

FIG. 3. Polarization Rotation. Coincidence response across the stripy-to-AFM phase transition of Fig. 1(d) for photons rotated from the input polarization. (a) Circularly polarized photons input with right (R) and output with left (L) circular polarization. (b) Linearly polarized photons input aligned with Kitaev z bonds (H) and output perpendicular to them (V ). Coincidence vs linearly polarized output rotation angle α relative to input light along the z bond for the AFM point at ϕ = 1.708π corresponding to the gray dotted line in (b), as the light-matter coupling strength D is scaled by (c) 1.0, (d) 0.5, and (e) 0.1 relative to its value in Fig. 1 and in Sec. FIG. 4. Model and Biasing Info. (a) yz(x), zx(y), and xy(z) bonds of the J-K-Γ model shown in blue, green, and red respectively on the 24 site cluster with periodic bound- ary conditions. (b) Phase diagram of the J-K-Γ model with Γ > 0, parameterized by θ and ϕ as in Eq. (A2). The blue line shows the linecut used to study the stripy-to- antiferromagnetic (AFM) quantum phase transition (QPT). (c) Strength of the matrix elements from the ground state to Raman active excitations across the stripy-to-AFM QPT. The orange dotted lines show the finite size splitting of the ground state manifold; this lines up with the Raman active low energy excitations in the stripy phase, but sits at higher...

FIG. 5. Light-Matter Coupling Parameters. D11 xx along the xy(z) bonds (a) as calculated from quasi-degenerate per- turbation theory going from the full to the spin Hamiltonian where possible (fitting errors above 10−6 appear in black) vs (b) using a least squares best fit against J, K, and Γ. (c) and (d) are the same for D11 zz.

Spintronics.note: The study directly links cavity quantum electrodynamics (Cavity QED) concepts—specifically using the optical cavity and photon statistics ($g^{(2)}$)—to probe magnetic properties derived from extended spin models like the Kitaev-Heisenberg system.
